ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb chop entry; no specific chump-cut or lean-grilled variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific chump chop lean grilled entry; A00LK (lamb meat, cooked) is the closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for grilled lean lamb chump chop without risk of fabrication.

serving.description: 1 lean grilled lamb chump chop (bone removed, fat trimmed)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lean grilled lamb chump chop is approximately 120 g, representing one chop with bone and all separable fat removed after grilling.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, loin chops, lean, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17174
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy 229 kcal, protein 34.1 g, fat 10.2 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g, sugar 0.0 g, sodium 65 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, potassium 330 mg, calcium 11 mg, iron 3.1 mg, cholesterol 93 mg) are taken from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06050007, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17174, lamb loin chop lean cooked broiled) is cited as the approved secondary cross-reference for saturated fat (4.1 g/100 g) and ruminant trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) values not separately reported in HPB Foodo. HPB Foodo is the primary source; USDA SR Legacy is the secondary approved source for fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lean lamb chump chop contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) confirm that pure protein and fat foods with negligible carbohydrate content are not assigned a GI value. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category is set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = not calculable; set to 0.0 by convention.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (65 mg/100 g) with high potassium (330 mg/100 g) yielding a strongly favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: The sodium content of 65 mg/100 g is well within DASH dietary targets, and the high potassium at 330 mg/100 g actively supports vasodilation; individuals with hypertension may include this food freely provided no high-sodium marinades or sauces are added at preparation or service.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein (34.1 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, this lean grilled lamb chop produces no postprandial glucose rise and is consistent with ADA dietary guidance for blood glucose management; portion size should be moderated to manage total energy and saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.1 g/100 g) and cholesterol (93 mg/100 g) present; ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) detected
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Although this is a lean cut with separable fat removed, residual saturated fat and ruminant-origin trans fat (vaccenic/conjugated linoleic acid) are present; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 85–100 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables and omega-3-rich foods to partially offset the lipid burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in red meat drives pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb is a red meat containing arachidonic acid that promotes RA-relevant inflammatory cascades, and the presence of ruminant trans fat further elevates inflammatory risk; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis are advised to substitute with oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) to obtain anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids, and if lamb is consumed, to pair it with anti-inflammatory sides such as turmeric-spiced vegetables or leafy greens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Lamb Chump Chop, Lean is a high-protein red meat dish prepared from the rear chump portion of the lamb carcass. The chump is a well-exercised muscle group situated between the loin and the leg, yielding a moderately tender cut with a robust, slightly gamey flavour characteristic of lamb. For this preparation, the bone is removed and all separable surface and intermuscular fat is trimmed away before or after grilling, leaving lean muscle meat only. The chop is grilled at high heat until cooked through, developing a slightly charred, caramelised exterior via Maillard browning while retaining a juicy interior. Per 100 g, this lean preparation delivers 229 kcal, 34.1 g protein, 10.2 g fat, 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 65 mg sodium, and 330 mg potassium. The very high protein content supports muscle maintenance and satiety. The zero carbohydrate profile means no glycaemic impact, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 65 mg/100 g, consistent with DASH dietary targets. Cholesterol is 93 mg/100 g and residual saturated fat is present, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size. Iron content of 3.1 mg/100 g contributes meaningfully to daily iron requirements, particularly haem iron which is highly bioavailable. This food is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a main protein component alongside non-starchy vegetables, salads, or grain-based sides. In Singapore and multicultural contexts, grilled lamb chops appear in mamak-style restaurants, Western-style eateries, and home cooking. The food is not a traditional Singaporean dish and does not carry mandatory multilingual naming requirements under Veritas protocol.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06050007, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat and trans fat fractions cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17174), an approved secondary source. Food identity is unambiguous and the primary source match is exact.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06050007); this is naturally zero as lamb muscle meat contains no digestible carbohydrate. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure animal muscle meat product with no plant-derived components. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol. GI source cited is Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care, a peer-reviewed journal, confirming that pure protein-fat foods with negligible carbohydrate are not assigned a GI value. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 4.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lean lamb (NDB 17174) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Trans_fat_g estimated at 0.2 g/100 g reflecting naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for lean cooked lamb; this is not industrially produced trans fat. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not separately confirmed from an approved source without risk of over-estimation. Water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo source record for this food item. This food is classified as Western/Generic and does not qualify as a Singaporean dish; therefore mandatory multilingual naming in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi is not required under Veritas G6 protocol. Primary cuisine tag of mamak from the input record is retained in meal_context as a contextual tag only; the food itself is a generic Western grilled meat preparation.
